frameworks.name: economic decision-making under uncertainty
frameworks.one_line: Distinguishes choices under risk (known probabilities) from ambiguity (unknown probabilities)

frameworks.name: cumulative lifetime stress exposure
frameworks.one_line: Aggregated major stressors across the lifespan as an individual-difference factor

frameworks.name: individual differences in uncertainty preferences
frameworks.one_line: Prior work explains risk attitudes; ambiguity aversion remains less characterized

core_question: Does cumulative lifetime stress exposure predict individual differences in ambiguity aversion?
key_assumptions: STRAIN inventory validly indexes cumulative lifetime stress exposure
Standard economic tasks dissociate risk from ambiguity preferences
Stress effects on uncertainty preferences are stable individual differences
manipulations: Measured cumulative lifetime stress with the STRAIN inventory
Separated early-life from total lifetime stress exposure
Quantified risk and ambiguity preferences via an economic choice task
key_findings: Greater lifetime stress exposure predicted higher ambiguity aversion
Effect was particularly pronounced for early-life stress exposure
No association observed between stress exposure and risk attitudes
Pattern held across two studies (n=58 and n=188)
interpretations: Cumulative life stress, especially early-life stress, selectively shapes aversion to ambiguity rather than risk
Lifetime stress is a candidate individual-difference factor explaining ambiguity aversion
